boundary: Public-safe portfolio and biographical material only. Never disclose private health, financial, relationship, family, compensation, manager, promotion, account, third-party, or unpublished Microsoft details.

chunks.title: About Aadith
chunks.body:
Product designer at Microsoft working on Copilot response UX, and co-founder of Owly, a brand-aware creative system for marketing teams. I grew up in Kerala, studied Design at IIT Guwahati, and have crossed India twice on a bicycle.

Designing AI people can inspect, direct, and actually use.

I grew up in Kerala and spent a lot of time drawing before I knew design was a job. At IIT Guwahati, that instinct widened into research, systems, product work, and a habit of learning whichever medium made the idea easier to experience. While studying, I also worked with early-stage teams across Web3, dashboards, personal branding, and fintech. Ambiguous briefs stopped feeling unusual quite early.

In 2024, I joined Microsoft to work on Copilot response UX. I focus on the parts around an AI answer: how it is structured, where the evidence appears, how people inspect sources, and how the team evaluates quality. It is a huge system built by many disciplines, so my job is less about having every answer and more about asking useful questions early enough to improve it.

In 2025, I started Owly with two friends from school. We began with one video problem, changed the buyer, changed the product, found early paid customers, and kept the useful insight: AI creative tools work better when they understand the brand and leave the steering wheel with the person using them. Some days that means product strategy; other days it means rewriting a button, fixing a demo, or discovering that our “simple” workflow contains twelve new states. Founder life has range.

Outside work, I cycle long distances. I have crossed India twice: an earlier northbound journey and, in 2025, a government-supported Kashmir-to-Kanyakumari expedition where I represented Kerala. The second covered roughly 4,000 km in 16 days. It taught me discipline, yes, but also that stubbornness works better with food, sleep, support, and people who tell you when you are being ridiculous.

I’m at my best when the problem is still foggy, the system has several owners, and the team needs both a clear story and something tangible to argue with.

chunks.title: How Aadith works
chunks.body:
Map the system before the screen: I begin with the actors, information, permissions, edge cases, and constraints. It is cheaper than beautifully polishing the wrong part of the system.

Turn taste into evidence: Interviews, customer conversations, examples, rubrics, and quality checks give a team something sturdier than “this feels better.” Judgment still matters; it just has to explain itself.

Make the trade-off visible: When a debate stays abstract, I build variants or a working prototype and attach a recommendation. The goal is not more options. It is a clearer decision.

Stay for the last mile: Accessibility, copy behavior, loading, spacing, and design-system fidelity often decide whether the idea survives implementation. I like being close enough to code to help.

chunks.title: Aadith's timeline
chunks.body:
2020 - IIT Guwahati - B.Des: Studied Design at IIT Guwahati. Learned that the interesting problems rarely fit inside one discipline.

2022 - Early-stage work began early: Worked across Web3 products, dashboards, identity systems, and client projects while still studying. Loose briefs and real deadlines were useful teachers.

2024 - Microsoft - Product Designer: Joined the Copilot team to work on how AI-generated responses are read, verified, and improved across Microsoft 365.

2025 - Owly - Co-founded: Started building a brand-aware creative system with two friends from school. Customer conversations became paid work, and paid work became sharper product questions.

2025 - Ride for Unity: Represented Kerala in a government-supported ride from Kashmir to Kanyakumari, covering roughly 4,000 km in 16 days with about 150 riders.

chunks.title: Cycling across India
chunks.body:
I've cycled across India twice. The first was a more independent journey from Kerala toward Kashmir while I was younger. The second, in 2025, was Kashmir to Kanyakumari, roughly 4,000 km in 16 days, as part of the government-supported Ride for Unity expedition. I represented Kerala among about 150 riders, with several stages stretching past 200 km.

At that distance, every day becomes surprisingly practical: eat, pedal, solve the next small problem, repeat. I learned how long a headwind can feel, how quickly confidence disappears when a climb appears around the corner, and how an impossible-looking route eventually becomes a collection of very ordinary kilometres.

I used to tell this story as proof of discipline. These days I think it is also proof that I am stubborn and had a good team around me. Design is not cycling, but both reward the same habit: stop dramatizing the whole distance and solve the next bit well.

Route: Kashmir to Kanyakumari. Distance: 4,000 km. Time: 16 days.

chunks.title: Contact Aadith
chunks.body:
Email: aadithva@outlook.com
LinkedIn: https://linkedin.com/in/aadithva
Behance: https://behance.net/aadithanil

chunks.title: Aadith's builder profile
chunks.body: Aadith V A is a product and interaction designer from Kerala, based in Hyderabad, and a Bachelor of Design graduate from IIT Guwahati. He joined Microsoft in August 2024 and works on Copilot response experiences involving citations, previews, references, and trust. He also co-founded Owly Studio, an AI video and creative-infrastructure venture, and has completed two India-scale cycling chapters. These practices reinforce one another: design gives him a method for observing, framing, testing, and refining; founder work adds urgency and ownership; endurance supplies a lived reference for long horizons and progress through repetition. A fair synthesis is that he is a high-agency builder who turns ambiguity into artifacts people can inspect: interfaces, prototypes, systems, stories, and working tools.

chunks.title: Education and early formation
chunks.body: Aadith studied for a Bachelor of Design at IIT Guwahati from 2020 to 2024. Double Diamond thinking became a durable foundation: widen the evidence, define the real problem, compare alternatives, prototype, and deliver. His final-year thesis explored personal financial guidance for India's middle-income population and included outreach to practicing financial advisers rather than relying only on secondary research. College already overlapped with real product work across Web3, dashboards, personal-branding products, and fintech, including public portfolio chapters for DaoLens, Crunch, Reachify, and Angel One. That early exposure to loose role boundaries helps explain why he is comfortable moving between research, interaction details, brand narrative, and implementation constraints.

chunks.title: Response UX and trustworthy AI
chunks.body: Aadith treats response UX as a connected system rather than one isolated feature. The design territory includes answer hierarchy, inline citations, hover or glance cards, document previews, follow-up actions, copy behavior, accessibility, and the transition from a generated claim into its source. Inline source pills make source identity recognizable near the relevant text, while the larger design tension is readability versus verifiability: evidence must be available without turning every answer into a wall of controls. Deep Citation continues the journey into the exact page or passage while preserving the user's conversational context. Related work considers whether text is an excerpt or a generated summary, how rich responses survive plain-text paste, what controls announce through a screen reader, and how local product needs align with a shared design system. Public material does not establish confidential rollout figures or impact metrics.

chunks.title: Aadith's AI-native design method
chunks.body: Aadith uses AI across a familiar design lifecycle rather than treating it as one magic step. Research still needs primary evidence; definition still depends on product data, stakeholder context, and real constraints; ideation and prototyping become cheaper through model-assisted exploration and code; delivery still returns to detailed specifications, redlines, accessibility, and engineering review. Synthetic personas can widen a team's questions, but they cannot validate a need or replace contact with real people. He increasingly builds interactive, imperfect prototypes before polishing static specifications because experienced behavior produces more honest feedback. AI also helps cluster messy critique and generate comparable variants, but the recommendation remains a human responsibility. His central caution is that abundant output raises the value of discernment: performance, security, data protection, edge cases, and last-mile craft do not become automatic.

chunks.title: How Owly evolved
chunks.body: Owly began with a child-focused idea for safer, more personal generated media, including parent-guided stories and visible learning context. In 2025 the team pivoted toward branded video for founders, growth teams, and larger product catalogs. The thread that survived the pivot is identity and context: generated media becomes less generic when it understands the brand and keeps an accountable human presence. The product promise is controlled acceleration, compressing a production process that can take weeks while preserving strategic and visual control. Aadith favors progressive editability: a few meaningful controls for beginners, followed by scene-level changes and selective regeneration when users need precision. The conceptual pipeline moves from brand understanding to strategy, scripts, visuals, assembly, approval, and distribution. The public portfolio reports early paid traction, but exact revenue, recurring revenue, and customer totals should not be inferred beyond the published figures.

chunks.title: Craft, storytelling, and collaboration
chunks.body: Aadith's toolkit spans product design, illustration, brand systems, motion, 3D, and code-based prototypes. He tends to choose the medium that makes an idea easiest to experience rather than protecting a narrow role boundary. He also thinks in scenes and sequences: hooks, transitions, scripts, product evidence, and calls to action are ways of making a complex system understandable over time. His preferred voice is direct, casual, and credible rather than polished into corporate anonymity. In collaboration, he often makes disagreement visible through concrete variants, then works toward a recommendation and decision criteria. His strongest recurring combination is synthesis, initiative, and narrative range: absorbing a foggy problem, finding a useful frame, making an artifact, and bringing it to other people early enough to improve it.

chunks.title: Two India-scale cycling chapters
chunks.body: Aadith has completed two distinct India-scale cycling chapters. The first was a self-directed, multi-week journey from Kerala toward Kashmir while he was a young adult; surviving accounts disagree on the exact distance and duration, so the public-safe description emphasizes the expedition rather than one disputed number. The second was the government-supported Ride for Unity from Kashmir to Kanyakumari in late 2025, with roughly 150 riders and Aadith representing Kerala. The common public version is about 4,000 kilometres in 16 days. The second ride was a supported national event and should not be described as solo. Together the rides show two forms of endurance: youthful self-direction in the first, and a much faster, structured team expedition in the second.

chunks.title: Owly
chunks.body:
I’m building Owly with two long-time friends: a brand-aware creative system that helps marketing teams reach a controllable video draft without sanding every brand down into the same AI look.

Role: Co-founder & CEO. Period: 2025–Present. Context: Owly.

Three friends, one very large idea

I started Owly with Adi and Hari, two friends I have known since school. I lead product, design, and much of the customer story; Adi works on the AI systems; Hari builds the backend and operational spine. The roles are complementary, even when the daily task list is not. We are building a creative system for marketing teams: bring in brand context, shape an idea, make a first video draft, refine it without starting over, and learn from what ships.

The product did not begin here

Owly first explored safer, more personal AI video for children. The emotional idea was strong, but the buyer, feedback loop, and economics were slow. In 2025, we moved toward brands and growth teams, where the need for frequent video was immediate and budgeted. The pivot changed the customer, not the useful insight: generated media becomes more valuable when it carries familiar identity, clear context, and meaningful control.

The relay race between brief and live

Creative teams often work on a slower clock than the platforms they feed. Ads fatigue, formats multiply, and a good brief gets stuck in a relay race across strategy, production, edits, approvals, and publishing. We kept seeing the same gap: the idea was not always the bottleneck; turning it into enough usable creative was.

A first draft you can actually direct

Owly reads a brand’s website and past creative, then uses that context to help generate a video draft. The useful part is not a shiny Generate button; it is what happens next. A marketer can respond in plain language, and the editor makes changes to the work on screen. We are still improving the loop, but the goal stays simple: make the first draft faster without taking direction away from the person responsible for the brand.

The product thesis

Context first. Control second. Generation somewhere in the middle.

The system we are working toward is less a model wrapper and more a repeatable creative workflow. Four ideas keep the product honest.

Learn the brand once: Bring the website, product catalog, tone, and past creative into a reusable context layer instead of restarting from a blank prompt.
Show the plan before the render: Use scripts, storyboards, progress, and approval points so a long generation step never feels like a sealed box.
Change a scene, not the universe: Support selective edits and regeneration. Full reruns are expensive in time, compute, and patience.
Make the next brief smarter: Carry customer feedback and performance context forward so repeat production improves instead of merely repeating.

The bet we’re making

Our long-term bet is that brand context, product catalogs, past creative, and performance history become reusable infrastructure. The hands-on service layer helps us learn and earn now; the company becomes more interesting when each customer improves a repeatable system rather than creating another bespoke workflow. We are not there yet. That is the work.

Founder titles are suspiciously broad

I lead product design, brand, and founder-led storytelling, but not in a vacuum. Adi pushes the AI architecture, Hari carries backend and operational execution, customers puncture our assumptions, and the work changes weekly. My part ranges from interaction flows and prototypes to the website, sales demos, and the words an AI editor uses when it needs clarification. It is less “owning every surface” and more keeping the many surfaces in the same conversation.

Co-founders: 3
Early paid customers: 8+
Company: Self-funded
Service-to-software bridge: Hybrid

Paid work is product research with consequences

We used early customer projects to learn where a polished generation demo stops being a usable workflow: inputs arrive messy, brand rules conflict, approvals take time, and one weak scene should not force a full rerender. Doing some of the work by hand is not the final model. It is how we learn which repeated decisions deserve to become software.

Under the hood, for the curious

The product uses Next.js, React, and Tailwind on the frontend, with FastAPI and Supabase behind it. A Python pipeline coordinates video, voice, and language models; brand context lives in Pinecone; and the editor builds on the open-source Friction project. The stack has changed more than once and will probably change again. We try to keep the product idea steadier than the model menu.

We are not trying to win a generation demo. We are trying to build a tool a creative team would genuinely miss when it is gone.

Aadith V A, Co-founder

chunks.title: Microsoft Copilot
chunks.body:
Making Copilot answers easier to read, question, verify, and use — through citations, response design, UX evaluation, accessibility, and the occasional detour into code.

Role: Product Designer. Period: 2024–Present. Context: Microsoft.

Public-safe version: I’ve kept the thinking and left out confidential product visuals, metrics, and implementation details.

AI can sound certain. People still deserve receipts.

My role is one small part of a very large team effort: shaping the experience around an AI-generated answer. I work with designers, product managers, engineers, researchers, quality specialists, and accessibility partners to make those answers easier to scan, trace back to evidence, evaluate, and improve. I do not make Copilot alone — thankfully. I focus on the moments where the model meets a person, because that is often where trust is earned or lost.

My part of the puzzle

Less magic. More clarity.

I keep returning to four practical habits. None is flashy on its own, but together they help an AI response feel less like a polished black box and more like something a person can understand, question, and use.

Make it scannable: Give complex responses a clear rhythm so the answer, evidence, and next action do not compete for attention.
Bring receipts: Keep source evidence close enough to a claim that verification feels natural, not like a second task.
Turn taste into a test: Convert “something feels off” into criteria, examples, and checks a wider team can actually reuse.
Mind the last 10%: Use prototypes and small code fixes to protect the accessibility and craft that can disappear during handoff.

Product world: Microsoft 365
My lane: Response UX
Recurring question: Can I verify this?
Often between: Figma ↔ code

01 — “Nice answer. Where did that come from?”

Generated answers arrive quickly; trust usually takes another beat. I worked on citation and reference patterns that connect claims to source material without turning every response into a legal appendix. The goal was quiet confidence: show evidence near the claim, reveal enough context to judge it, and keep the full source one step away.

A verification ladder

Let the evidence deepen only when the person asks.

A citation is not one component. It is a sequence that should preserve reading flow while giving someone a credible path from a claim to its basis.

Recognize the source: Keep a compact, readable source identity near the relevant claim so the answer does not become a field of controls.
Inspect useful context: Reveal an excerpt or preview that helps someone judge relevance without abandoning the answer.
Open the exact evidence: When the stakes are higher, carry the person into the source context instead of making them search a long document again.
Keep meaning after reuse: Preserve structure, source clarity, and accessible semantics when content is copied, read aloud, or moved into a less capable surface.

02 — AI responses collect a lot of furniture

Citations, actions, cards, previews, artifacts — useful pieces can still become a crowded room. I contributed to a wider team effort to modernize how those pieces fit together, simplifying layout and hierarchy so people could find the answer, the evidence, and the next action without decoding the interface first. Most of the work happened in critique, edge cases, and the stubborn details that only appear when a concept meets a real product.

03 — Taste is useful. A repeatable quality bar is better.

Designers are good at saying, “Something feels off.” That does not scale especially well. As a named designer on a UX evaluation initiative, I helped turn qualitative judgment into reusable criteria, labeled examples, rubrics, and prompt-based checks. The framework became operational and surfaced quality issues across multiple response experiences — giving the team a way to catch problems earlier and discuss them with more than vibes.

04 — Pixels have a habit of wandering

Even a careful specification can arrive in the product slightly… sideways. I use code and near-production prototypes to test interactions and contribute small, low-risk presentation-layer fixes. Accessibility, spacing, typography, and component behavior are not always glamorous, but they are often the difference between a design that looks complete and one that feels considered.

Good work survives a lot of questions

Formal accessibility reviews, design-spec reviews, critique, and cross-functional checks rarely produce a dramatic portfolio thumbnail. They do produce better decisions. I contribute to those review layers, raise questions early, and lean on partners who see risks or constraints I do not. The point is not to be the person with every answer; it is to help the team notice the right problems.

Sharing the messy middle

Outside feature work, I helped run an internal design-enablement initiative about using AI across research, synthesis, prototyping, evaluation, and implementation. I organized recurring sessions, demos, and supporting website work — not as a guru with a perfect method, but as a curious practitioner documenting what worked, what did not, and what others might borrow.

What I am still figuring out

Plenty. How much evidence is reassuring before it becomes clutter? When should an evaluation automate a check, and when should a person keep the final say? How do patterns stay coherent across products without becoming rigid? I am still working through those questions with people who know different parts of the system better than I do. That uncertainty is not a footnote to the work; it is the interesting part.

How I tend to show up

I ask a lot of small questions: Is the evidence close enough to the claim? Can someone scan this in five seconds? What happens with an odd source? Did the accessible version keep the same meaning? Can we test this judgment instead of debating it forever? I work best in the messy middle between design, research, quality, and engineering, where the answer usually gets better when nobody pretends to know everything.

I’m not trying to make AI look all-knowing. I’m trying to make it easier for people to know when it’s useful.

Aadith V A

chunks.title: Angel One
chunks.body:
A short fintech design role near the end of college — and my first close look at how trust, legibility, and hesitation behave inside a large financial product.

Role: UX Design. Period: Jan–Jun 2024. Context: Angel One.

Public-safe role summary: detailed product artifacts are not included here, and I am not attaching a shipped metric or feature claim that I cannot verify.

A short role, and a useful change of scale

Near the end of my final year, I spent roughly six months doing UX work in Angel One’s product environment. I do not have a polished Behance board for this one, and the surviving public artifacts are thinner than the work itself, so I am keeping the story appropriately modest. What I can stand behind is the context: a large financial-services product, high-stakes information, and people who need to understand what they are doing before they tap.

The useful overlap with my thesis

The role overlapped with my final academic semester and research around financial advising. That made the work feel less like a sudden domain switch and more like theory meeting a product with real scale. Finance interfaces do not get to hide uncertainty behind a cheerful button. Labels, hierarchy, confirmation, and edge cases carry more weight because the cost of misunderstanding is not abstract.

What the context sharpened

Trust is usually made from small, unglamorous decisions

I am not presenting these as patented fintech principles. They are the practical habits the environment reinforced.

Make the decision legible: A person should understand what they are choosing, what changes next, and where to inspect the detail before committing.
Design for hesitation: Pauses, comparison, and second thoughts are not friction to bulldoze. In financial products, they are often signs that the interface needs to explain more clearly.
Keep hierarchy honest: Primary actions should look primary, but risk, cost, and constraints should not be visually negotiated into fine print.
Hand off the edge cases: A neat happy path is not enough. States, validation, and exception behaviour need enough detail to survive implementation.

No inflated impact slide

I could make this page sound larger by borrowing the language of shipped impact, but I do not have the primary material to support that claim here. The honest value of Angel One in my story is the bridge it created: from early startup work and academic finance research into a much larger, more consequential product system.

Timeline: 6 months
Domain: Retail finance
Context: Final semester
Public claim: Intentionally narrow

High-stakes products do not reward decorative certainty. They reward interfaces that help people understand what happens next.

Aadith V A

chunks.title: Reachify
chunks.body:
An AI writing and scheduling companion built for one job: helping busy professionals show up consistently on LinkedIn without spending their evenings on it.

Role: Freelance Product Designer. Period: 2023. Context: Reachify — early-stage startup.

A personal brand is a lot of small, repeated effort

Reachify started from a familiar problem: people know they should post on LinkedIn, then reach the end of a full workday with no idea what to say. Over four months, I worked solo across research, information architecture, UI, and a working AI-assisted prototype. The aim was not to manufacture a personal brand overnight. It was to make the small, recurring work of finding an idea, writing it well, scheduling it, and learning from it feel manageable.

First, find the people already doing the work

I screened for active LinkedIn creators with roughly 2,000–10,000 followers — enough experience to have real habits, but not so much that a team was already doing the work for them. More than 100 cold messages led to 15–20 interviews. The themes were practical rather than glamorous: inconsistent ideas, uncertainty about the algorithm, too many disconnected tools, limited time, and a vague hope that expertise might eventually turn into income.

Product structure

One product, four recurring jobs

The research became a product organised around the work people repeated every week, rather than a dashboard full of impressive-looking AI buttons.

Create: Generate an idea, shape the writing with tone and category controls, add hashtags, and schedule the finished post.
Engage: Understand who is responding, where a conversation is growing, and which relationships may be worth continuing.
Measure: Read follower, impression, and post-level trends without turning every creative decision into a spreadsheet.
Return: Use one dashboard to pick up the next small task instead of rebuilding the workflow across several tools.

Two days is not a lot of time

Partway through the project, the investor timeline compressed into a two-day MVP sprint for onboarding and the Create module. Analytics and Engagement became honest “coming soon” states while the core flow had to work. It was not the process I would plan on purpose, but it clarified the product quickly: if idea → write → schedule was not useful, the rest of the roadmap was decoration.

Doing the job the product was built for

Alongside the product, I wrote and designed content for Reachify’s own LinkedIn page — hooks, carousel posts, storytelling formats, and practical templates. The page grew to 2,000 followers. I liked the symmetry of that: the fastest way to understand a tool for consistent posting was to become one of the people consistently posting.

Timeline: 4 months
Screens: 30+
Interviews: 15–20
LinkedIn growth: 2,000

I too face this problem of not knowing what to post and how. Have been trying to build a profile that catches eyeballs and ReachifyMe has been a very helpful tool.

Early Reachify user

chunks.title: DaoLens
chunks.body:
Early startup design across product, brand, and go-to-market — with a two-week DAO Denver sprint as the clearest surviving public artifact.

Role: Product, brand & marketing design. Period: 2022–2023. Context: DaoLens.

The startup where the brief rarely stayed in one lane

I joined DaoLens at around twenty and worked across a young Web3 company where product, brand, and go-to-market problems regularly arrived in the same week. The surviving records point to onboarding, contributor dashboards, governance-oriented experiences, marketing, and visual systems. My exact title changes across old drafts, so I prefer the less dramatic truth: I was an early designer doing whatever the product and company needed, often before the boundaries were tidy.

My working range

Product, brand, and go-to-market kept borrowing from one another

The useful lesson was not that I could do several kinds of design. It was seeing how decisions in one layer created constraints in the others.

Product: Onboarding, contributor dashboards, access, and governance-oriented experiences had to explain unfamiliar Web3 concepts without assuming every user already spoke the language.
Brand: The visual system needed enough technical credibility for a B2B product while remaining flexible across community and marketing moments.
Go-to-market: Events, social content, product explainers, and campaigns translated the product into reasons for someone to stop, understand, and continue the conversation.
Startup rhythm: The brief moved quickly and ownership was broad. I learned to make progress without waiting for every role boundary to become official.

The public record is uneven, so the structure is honest

The DAO Denver sprint has a complete Behance trail and now lives as its own case study above. The broader product work needs a careful artifact pass before I describe individual flows in detail. I would rather leave a clear gap than fill it with a confident reconstruction.

Period: 2022–2023
Environment: Early startup
Working range: Product → brand
Detailed chapter: DAO Denver

DaoLens was where I learned that an early designer does not get to protect a tidy job description from the needs of the company.

Aadith V A

chunks.title: Crunch
chunks.body:
Dashboard and data-visualisation work for an analytics product — and an early lesson in making complex information readable without pretending I was the data scientist.

Role: Dashboard & information design. Period: 2022–2023. Context: CrunchIt.

The job was to explain the data, not invent it

Crunch brought me into dashboard and data-visualisation work while I was still moving between college, startup, and freelance projects. The contribution sat at the interface layer: organise dense information, make comparisons easier, and help a person find the signal without flattening everything into one heroic number.

I said the quiet part out loud

In related project conversations, I was clear that I was not deeply trained in data science. I had dashboard experience, curiosity, and the willingness to learn the domain; I did not have a reason to pretend I had built the underlying models. That boundary made the design work better. It kept my attention on the part I could own: how evidence, comparison, status, and uncertainty reached the reader.

Information-design checklist

A useful dashboard owes the reader four things

The visuals may change with the domain. These questions survive surprisingly well.

A starting point: The page needs a clear first read before it asks someone to inspect charts, filters, and secondary metrics.
A fair comparison: Labels, scales, time ranges, and baselines should make comparison easier rather than quietly steering the conclusion.
Visible uncertainty: Incomplete or ambiguous data should look incomplete or ambiguous. Confidence is part of the information architecture.
A next question: A dashboard becomes more useful when it helps someone move from observation to the next sensible investigation.

A small project with a long afterlife

The work was not as broad as Owly or as deeply documented as Reachify. Its influence is quieter. The same information-design muscle now shows up when I work on citations, source panels, evaluation reports, and AI outputs that need to communicate evidence without acting more certain than they are.

Focus: Dashboards
Contribution: Visualisation
Domain: Analytics
Model work: Not claimed

My job was not to make the data look impressive. It was to help someone read it without getting lost.

Aadith V A

chunks.title: Citations
chunks.body:
Making source identity visible inside an AI answer without turning every paragraph into a wall of references.

Area: Response UX / source grounding. Period: 2025–Present. Status: documented.

Public-safe summary: internal product visuals, metrics, names, and implementation details are intentionally omitted.

A tiny component with system-sized consequences

A citation has to do several jobs at once. It should tell a reader that evidence exists, help them recognise the source, stay close to the claim, and avoid interrupting the answer so often that the answer becomes hard to read. My work explored that balance across inline references, multiple-source cases, hover states, accessibility, dark mode, and the transition into deeper source context.

Design questions

Readable enough to continue. Verifiable enough to trust.

The useful design work lived in the trade-offs, not in decorating a pill.

Recognition: Use a source name, type, or familiar marker so a reference means more than an abstract number.
Proximity: Keep evidence close enough to the claim that verification feels like a continuation of reading, not a separate research task.
Escalation: Let compact references open into excerpts, previews, and exact source context as the user asks for more.
Edge cases: Design for truncation, multiple sources, lists, tables, keyboard access, contrast, and narrow layouts before calling the component finished.

The part I keep returning to

The best citation is neither invisible nor dominant. It appears when confidence needs support, then gets out of the way. That sounds simple until one answer contains files, web pages, messages, meetings, mixed source mappings, and a person using only a keyboard.

chunks.title: Deep citations
chunks.body:
Taking someone from a generated claim to the exact place in a long source document, without losing the conversation they came from.

Area: Verification / document preview. Period: 2025–Present. Status: documented.

Public-safe summary: the interaction model is described at a high level; confidential screens, rollout details, and internal data are excluded.

Opening a file is not the same as finding the evidence

A normal link can take someone to a document and still leave them hunting through dozens of pages. Deep citation explored a more continuous verification path: preserve the answer, open the source beside it, move to the relevant page or section, and make the cited material easier to locate.

Interaction model

Claim → context → exact evidence

Each layer should answer the next question without forcing a reset.

Claim: Start from the statement the user is already reading and keep its source relationship visible.
Context: Use an excerpt or preview to show enough surrounding material for the user to judge whether opening the source is worthwhile.
Exact location: Navigate to the relevant page, section, table, chart, or source fragment rather than dropping the reader at the document cover.
Return path: Keep the conversation available so verification does not destroy the task the user was trying to complete.

Why this mattered to me

The interaction treats trust as a journey rather than a badge. An AI answer can be wrong with beautiful typography. The interface becomes more useful when it reduces the cost of checking, especially in long enterprise files where the evidence may be a paragraph, a slide, a table, or several fragments working together.

chunks.title: Meetings
chunks.body:
A dedicated chapter for meeting-related response UX: recaps, highlights, source identity, and the awkward question of what still matters after the call ends.

Area: Meeting intelligence / response UX. Period: 2025–Present. Status: draft.

Documentation in progress. This page currently sets the public-safe frame; product-specific screens and internal details are not included.

Meetings are sources with a timeline

A document sits still. A meeting unfolds through speakers, decisions, corrections, and unfinished actions. Designing AI experiences around that material means preserving who said what, distinguishing a recap from evidence, and helping someone recover the moment that made a summary meaningful.

What belongs in this chapter

From a long call to useful, inspectable context

This folder is ready for the individual workstreams and artifacts as they are cleared for the portfolio.

Recap and highlights: How the system chooses, groups, and presents the moments worth returning to.
Meeting as a source: How a meeting is identified alongside files, messages, email, and web references.
Attribution: How speakers, timestamps, and surrounding conversation help a generated statement remain checkable.
Follow-through: How decisions and actions survive the transition from the meeting into the next piece of work.

chunks.title: System messages
chunks.body:
Designing the hidden instruction layer as product behaviour: tone, boundaries, recovery, and what the system should do when the happy path runs out.

Area: Agent behaviour / content systems. Period: 2025–Present. Status: draft.

Documentation in progress. No internal prompt text, policies, or confidential implementation details are published here.

The invisible interface still has users

System messages sit behind the UI, but their decisions appear everywhere: how the assistant explains limits, asks for missing context, handles uncertainty, cites evidence, and recovers after a mistake. I want this page to document that work as interaction design rather than treating it as a mysterious block of prompt text.

Behaviour layer

Instructions are only useful when their effects can be inspected

The eventual case study will connect authored guidance to the behaviour a user actually experiences.

Intent: State the job the system is trying to do and the priorities that should survive edge cases.
Boundaries: Make limits and prohibited behaviour explicit without turning every response into defensive boilerplate.
Recovery: Describe what the assistant should do when context is missing, tools fail, or the user corrects it.
Evaluation: Pair instructions with scenarios and observable quality criteria so behaviour can be reviewed rather than merely hoped for.

chunks.title: Turn N
chunks.body:
The first answer gets the demo. Turn N gets the product: follow-ups, corrections, remembered context, and whether the conversation still makes sense later.

Area: Multi-turn conversation / continuity. Period: 2025–Present. Status: draft.

Documentation in progress. This page describes the design problem without exposing internal prompts, scenarios, or implementation details.

A good first turn can hide a fragile conversation

AI demos are usually kind to the first answer. Real use is not. People refine the request, refer to something from three turns ago, change direction, correct the assistant, and expect the system to understand which parts should remain. Turn N is the chapter for that less photogenic but more important work.

Continuity checks

What should survive as the conversation gets longer?

The case study will collect scenarios, decisions, and evaluation criteria around multi-turn behaviour.

Reference: Resolve what “that,” “the second one,” or “use the earlier file” actually points to.
Correction: Let new information update the conversation without repeating the same mistake in a nicer sentence.
Constraint: Carry forward the rules that still matter and release the ones the user has intentionally changed.
Recovery: When continuity fails, ask a useful clarifying question instead of confidently inventing the missing link.

chunks.title: Hackathons & internal builds
chunks.body:
Fast experiments, culture-making, and small internal tools — useful work that does not belong inside the main product case study.

Area: Rapid prototyping / culture. Period: 2024–Present. Status: growing.

Growing collection. Individual projects will be added only when names, collaborators, ownership, and public-safe artifacts are confirmed.

A folder for the work that happened sideways

Hackathons and internal builds gave me room to move between product prototypes, storytelling, brand, and culture. The outcomes range from practical tools to visual systems and event moments. They deserve their own chapter because squeezing them into the Copilot product narrative makes both stories less clear.

Documentation rule

Every project should earn its own page

I will add individual hackathon entries here as the evidence is organised. Each one needs the same basic honesty.

Brief: What problem or internal moment the team was trying to address.
My part: What I personally designed, built, wrote, organised, or helped ship — with collaborators named.
Artifact: A public-safe screenshot, prototype, identity, video frame, or implementation detail that proves the work existed.
Afterlife: Whether the idea shipped, taught us something, won recognition, became a resource, or simply ended when the hackathon did.

chunks.title: DAO Denver
chunks.body:
A two-week sprint to turn a sleek B2B Web3 product into a booth people might actually stop at — designed together with Ans K James.

Area: Event identity / go-to-market. Period: 2023. Status: documented.

Eight deliverables. Two weeks. One trade floor.

DaoLens was sponsoring a booth at DAO Denver during ETHDenver 2023. The request covered the booth, banners, social posts, stickers, merchandise, posters, pamphlets, a website, and a paper game. The Behance project credits Ans K James and me together without splitting every deliverable by person, so I present it here as a shared build — not a solo identity project with a collaborator added in the footer.

Loud, on purpose

DaoLens already had a futuristic, gradient-heavy identity built for a B2B product. A trade floor needed a different volume. The event direction became bolder, brighter, flatter, and more playful — Space Mono and Poppins, blocky illustrations, and a sharp yellow accent — while keeping enough of the original brand that the booth still belonged to DaoLens.

Event system

The same idea had to survive eight formats

A booth identity only works when it still behaves at the size of a sticker, a screen, a shirt, and a very large banner.

Stop: A booth, banners, and a dartboard merch game gave people a visible reason to pause.
Explain: Pamphlets and website mockups translated the DAO Manager product into something visitors could understand quickly.
Travel: Stickers, shirts, mugs, notebooks, and tote bags let the visual language leave the booth.
Continue: Social posts carried panel discussions and event activations into the wider ETHDenver conversation.

The game nobody expected at a DAO booth

Alongside the expected merch, the system included a paper fortune-teller with deliberately silly Web3 copy. The joke was not there to prove we could write jokes. It gave someone walking past a low-stakes way to touch the brand, smile, and stay for the product conversation.

Timeline: 2 weeks
Event: 3 days
Formats: 8
Collaboration: With Ans

chunks.title: FlowSense
chunks.body:
A screen-recording reviewer that turns a user flow into frame-level evidence, scores, and a cautious quality gate.

A screenshot can show that a screen looks off. It cannot show whether feedback arrived late, a user backtracked, or an error quietly swallowed the flow. Manual review can catch those things, but it takes time and varies with the reviewer.

This is the most complete example of how I like to work: start with a fuzzy UX judgement, turn it into observable criteria, and build enough system around the rubric that the result can be inspected, challenged, and repeated.

FlowSense began with a design question: could a recorded user flow be reviewed against a consistent rubric without pretending the score was objective truth? I wanted the speed of automation, but not the false confidence of a mysterious number.

The project grew from a local model experiment into a monorepo with an upload interface, video processor, shared schemas, storage, frame analysis, and a report. The useful architectural shift was two-pass inference: first describe the observable interaction facts, then score those facts against the rubric. “What happened?” and “Was it good?” should not be the same prompt.

I kept adding places for the system to show its work. Issues point back to frames. Repeat runs show regressions. Truncated analysis can warn but not pass. The result is not a replacement for design review; it is a way to arrive at review with a consistent first pass and a trail of receipts.

A seven-category weighted rubric produces a 0–100 score and a pass, warn, or block quality gate.
The processor extracts video frames, selects meaningful changes, and separates fact extraction from rubric scoring.
Reports link issues and recommendations back to source frames, then compare repeat runs for regressions.
Signed webhooks, nonce replay protection, managed identity, and atomic SQL claims harden the processing path.
The monorepo includes targeted tests, CI gates, shared schemas, and an evaluation harness for agreement and release metrics.

Limitation: It depends on configured Azure services and credentials, so it is not a one-click public demo.
Limitation: Authentication is deliberately bypassed in the local-development path and must be re-enabled for deployment.
Limitation: The benchmark machinery exists, but the repository does not include a populated human-labelled holdout set.
Limitation: Long recordings can hit the analysis budget; when that happens the system downgrades the quality gate instead of bluffing.
Limitation: The model can surface evidence and apply a rubric, but a designer still has to judge whether the rubric fits the product.

The biggest improvements came from structuring the evidence, not simply swapping models. Separating “what happened?” from “how well did it happen?” made the analysis easier to reason about — and easier to distrust in useful ways.

Built with: Turbo monorepo, Next.js 15, TypeScript, React 18, Azure SQL, Azure Blob, Azure OpenAI, Entra ID, Express worker, ffmpeg.

chunks.title: Figma Redline Bridge
chunks.body:
A local bridge that lets an AI or script draw measurements, specs, and annotation batches inside Figma.

An external agent can reason about a design, but Figma plugins live in a sandbox and wait for a person to click things. I wanted a safe, inspectable bridge between those two worlds — mostly so I could stop drawing the same spacing arrows by hand.

This project connects design judgement to working infrastructure. It is not an AI wrapper around a button; it is a small protocol, relay, plugin runtime, and set of Figma-native annotation primitives that have to agree with one another.

The first version answered a very small question: could I ask an external tool to draw a spacing redline in the Figma file I already had open? Figma's sandbox makes the answer more interesting than it sounds.

I ended up with a local relay. An HTTP request becomes a WebSocket message, crosses the plugin iframe through postMessage, and finally reaches the Figma API. Replies make the same trip back. On top of that plumbing I added spacing, dimension, colour, typography, page, and batch commands.

The project later grew into broader documentation experiments, but the bridge is still the part I like most. It automates repetition without claiming to automate taste. The person still decides what deserves an annotation; the tool just saves the small ceremony of drawing it.

A four-hop command path connects HTTP clients to a Node relay, WebSocket, plugin iframe, and Figma sandbox.
Remote commands cover spacing, dimensions, colour, typography, page creation, and ordered batches.
The server queues commands while Figma is disconnected and exposes connection state through a health endpoint.
The plugin preserves auto-layout by rescaling cloned frames rather than mutating dimensions directly.

Limitation: The localhost command endpoint is unauthenticated and should not be exposed beyond a trusted local machine.
Limitation: Only one active Figma client is supported; a new connection replaces the previous socket.
Limitation: Batch commands fail when Figma is disconnected instead of joining the normal pending-command queue.
Limitation: Setup still requires a server process, a plugin build, and a manually loaded Figma development manifest.
Limitation: There is no automated test suite for the cross-runtime message path.

The hard part was not drawing a line. It was respecting three different runtimes, Figma's plugin sandbox, and auto-layout without making the tool feel haunted. A boring protocol turned out to be the useful bit.

Built with: Figma plugin, TypeScript, Node.js, WebSocket (ws).

chunks.title: Behavior Diff
chunks.body:
A before-and-after viewer for the dangerous little phrase: “I only changed one prompt.”

A one-line change to a prompt, policy, or context can quietly rewrite an agent's whole response. Reading two walls of text side by side is a poor way to understand what actually moved.

This is a small prototype with a strong rule: change one variable at a time. That constraint turns a flashy before-and-after demo into something closer to an explainable behaviour experiment.

Agent changes can look tiny in configuration and enormous in the answer. I wanted the equivalent of a code diff for behaviour: not a score that says better or worse, but a view that shows where the response expanded, disappeared, or changed its mind.

The prototype ships with three controlled scenarios. One tightens the quality bar, one adds a VIP constraint, and one changes only the incoming message. A custom word-level diff engine feeds both split and unified views, so the comparison stays consistent whichever reading mode feels clearer.

The rerun is simulated for now. That is an important limitation, but it also kept the prototype honest: I could concentrate on the comparison grammar before spending time on an evaluation backend. The next real test is whether the same interface survives live, messy, non-deterministic runs.

Three scenarios isolate a quality-bar change, a new VIP constraint, and an escalated customer input.
A custom longest-common-subsequence engine computes word-level additions, removals, and unchanged spans.
Split and unified views share one diff result, with line alignment and empty rows keeping comparisons legible.
Change statistics, configuration deltas, and a simulated rerun state complete the intended evaluation loop.

Limitation: The outputs are authored fixtures; the rerun button does not call a live model.
Limitation: The scenarios cover one customer-support domain rather than a broader behaviour benchmark.
Limitation: Configuration is visible but not editable, so new experiments still require code changes.
Limitation: There is no backend, persistence, automated test suite, or committed deployment.

If I change three things and compare two outputs, I learn almost nothing. The useful design decision was not the red-and-green diff; it was forcing every scenario to state the single variable that changed.

Built with: Next.js 16, React 19, Tailwind CSS 4, Framer Motion.

chunks.title: Agent Canvas
chunks.body:
A Figma-ish canvas for specifying how an agent moves, decides, responds, and gets judged.

Agent behaviour usually disappears into prompts, configuration, and orchestration code. That makes the most important product decisions hard to see, critique, or hand to someone else.

This is my clearest attempt to treat agent behaviour as designed material. The interface is a prototype, but the underlying model — steps, decisions, responses, and weighted quality criteria — is the part I wanted to make discussable.

I kept running into the same awkward handoff: we could draw a user flow, write a prompt, or make a prototype, but none of those quite described how an agent should behave turn by turn. So I sketched a tool that borrows the spatial confidence of Figma without pretending a conversation is just a flowchart.

Each scenario is built from typed steps. Select one and the inspector shows its instruction, output, and quality criteria. Switch to Preview and the same structure becomes a small conversation replay, with the active message and canvas node lighting up together.

Some of that is deliberate theatre. The drag handles do not yet drag, and the preview is simulated. I built enough of the interaction to test the product idea first: can an agent spec feel tangible enough for a designer, PM, and engineer to point at the same thing?

Three sample scenarios model onboarding, file analysis, and error recovery as connected five-step flows.
Four step types — trigger, action, decision, and response — give the canvas a small but coherent behaviour vocabulary.
Quality criteria carry must, should, and nice-to-have weights rather than sitting in a separate checklist.
Preview mode replays a simulated conversation and cross-highlights the active canvas node.

Limitation: The preview currently replays one hard-coded scenario rather than compiling the flow being edited.
Limitation: Edits live only in local component state; refresh the page and they are gone.
Limitation: Dragging, adding steps, and some toolbar controls are visual promises, not implemented tools.
Limitation: There is no agent backend, persistence layer, automated test suite, or deployment yet.

A prototype can be useful before it is technically complete if it gives a team better nouns to argue with. The canvas made the missing pieces obvious too: persistence matters, but compiling an editable flow into a real preview matters more.

Built with: Next.js 16, React 19, Tailwind CSS 4, Framer Motion, Lucide React.

chunks.title: Conversation Pattern Library
chunks.body:
A searchable shelf of conversation patterns for when “just add chat” is not a design strategy.

Teams keep rediscovering the same conversational moves — clarify an ambiguous request, reveal complexity gradually, recover context, confirm a risky action — and then bury the reasoning inside individual mockups.

This is the smallest project in the shortlist, but it shows a habit I care about: turning repeated design judgement into a reusable system without sanding away the context that made the pattern useful.

I did not want to make another collection of polished chat bubbles. The more useful question was whether a designer could find the right conversational move for a particular moment, understand why it fits, and see how it unfolds over several turns.

The library currently holds six patterns: Clarification Loop, Progressive Disclosure, Graceful Fallback, Escalation to Human, Destructive Action Confirmation, and Context Recovery. Each uses the same schema, so search, category filters, anatomy diagrams, guidelines, and replayable examples all come from one source of truth.

It is intentionally a seed. Six patterns do not make a full design system, and the contribution model exists only in my head. But building the browser exposed the real next question: patterns should link to one another, because good conversations rarely use only one.

Six authored patterns span clarification, disclosure, error handling, handoff, and confirmation.
Every pattern includes when-to-use guidance, principles, four anatomy steps, a conversation example, and tags.
Search matches names, descriptions, and tags; category filters update the visible library count.
Live Preview, Anatomy, and Guidelines views let the same pattern be read as an example, a structure, or practical advice.

Limitation: The library currently contains six patterns, so it is a convincing seed rather than a comprehensive system.
Limitation: Patterns are static TypeScript data with no authoring, review, or contribution workflow.
Limitation: There are no deep links to a selected pattern, and browser history does not capture exploration.
Limitation: The composability note is promising, but related patterns are not yet modelled or navigable.

A pattern library is more than a gallery of good examples. It becomes useful when it explains selection, boundaries, and relationships. This version handles the first two; the next interesting job is making patterns compose.

Built with: Next.js 16, React 19, Tailwind CSS 4, Framer Motion.
